title: The Shock Waves of Iran: Navigating Energy Weaponization and Inflation
content:
The strategic weaponization of energy resources continues to send severe economic shock waves across the globe. Historically, when critical trade routes are threatened or access to essential fuels is restricted, nations suffer from soaring inflation, shortened workweeks, and sudden factory closures. During the energy crises of the 1970s, many policymakers concluded that international cooperation and market integration were the best defenses against such disruptions. However, in today's increasingly fragmented and conflict-prone world, many leaders are drawing the exact opposite conclusion. As a result, there is a growing consensus that reliance on global energy networks poses an unacceptable national security risk.

This drive to reduce exposure to volatile international markets has profoundly influenced the modern transition to clean energy. Governments are actively seeking alternative power sources that can be generated domestically to insulate their economies from foreign interference. Yet, as the demand for electric vehicles and advanced batteries surges, new vulnerabilities have emerged in the supply chains for rare-earth elements. When dominant suppliers intentionally restrict exports of these critical materials, it disrupts manufacturing and causes prices to skyrocket. Ultimately, achieving true energy independence will require nations to secure both their renewable energy infrastructure and the raw materials essential for its construction.
